coredata.dc:title: Sweet graphene quantum dots for imaging carbohydrate receptors in live cells
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jie
Than, Aung
Li, Nan
Ananthanarayanan, Arundithi
Zheng, Xinting
Xi, Fengna
Liu, Jiyang
Tian, Jingqi
Chen, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging zero-dimensional materials promising a spectrum of novel applications, for example, for bioimaging owing to their desirable properties including tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, good biocompatibility, and molecular size. In this study, carboxylated single-crystalline GQDs with high brightness are synthesized using a bottom-up approach. And for the first time, GQDs are conjugated with monosaccharide moieties and employed to reveal the distribution and dynamic trafficking of carbohydrate receptors on live cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cell imaging
Monosaccharides
Carbohydrate receptors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of GQDs (scale bar=10nm). High-resolution TEM image is shown in inset (scale bar=2nm). (b) Diameter distribution of GQDs (n=103). (c) AFM images of GQDs. Inset shows the height profile along the red line. Scale bar=50nm. (d) Height distribution of GQDs (n=157).
FTIR spectrum (a) and XPS analyses (b–e) of the prepared GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum. Inset is the photograph of the aqueous dispersion of GQDs under ambient light. (b) Photoluminescence excitation spectrum (PLE, emission at 535nm) and emission spectrum (PL, excited at 488nm) of GQDs. Inset: Aqueous dispersion of GQDs under a UV lamp illumination (365nm). (c) Energy band diagram. (d) Emission spectra of GQDs excited with different wavelengths.
Cell viability tests based on MTT assay.
(a) FTIR and (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs before and after conjugation with galactose and mannose.
Confocal fluorescence imaging of Gal-GQD and Man-GQD stained HepG2, MCF-7, HeLa and, MCF-10A cells. Scale bar=5μm.

(a) and (b) TIRF images of HepG2 cells applied with Gal-GQDs (scale bar=5μm). (c) and (d) Time-lapse TIRFM images of endocytosis (c) and exocytosis (d) of Gal-GQD labeled galactose receptors (ASGPR). (e) Schematic illustration of the trafficking and recycling of galactose receptors.
Synthetic route of sugar functionalized GQDs.

head.ce:title: Sweet graphene quantum dots for imaging carbohydrate receptors in live cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 637457, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, A*STAR (Agency for Science Technology and Research), 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering
A*STAR (Agency for Science Technology and Research)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Sciences, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging zero-dimensional materials promising a spectrum of novel applications, for example, for bioimaging owing to their desirable properties including tunable photoluminescence, high photostability, good biocompatibility, and molecular size. In this study, carboxylated single-crystalline GQDs with high brightness are synthesized using a bottom-up approach. And for the first time, GQDs are conjugated with monosaccharide moieties and employed to reveal the distribution and dynamic trafficking of carbohydrate receptors on live cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are atomically thin carbon sheet with a diameter typically <10nm, are emerging a new class of zero dimensional nanomaterials which promise a wide spectrum of applications including imaging [1,2], optical sensing [3–7], photocatalysis [8,9], energy storage and conversion [10,11], display [12,13], etc. In particular, GQDs have unique advantages in bioimaging because of several key merits including tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, high photo-stability, good biocompatibility, and molecular size [14]. Many studies have shown the feasibility of GQD based cellular imaging via non-specific cellular internalization of GQDs [15,16]. A few studies have demonstrated the specific imaging of proteinaceous membrane receptors in cells by functionalizing GQDs with corresponding ligand proteins.

Cell membrane is not only populated with proteins but also saccharides and their respective receptors which are involved in many biological processes including cellular signaling, recognition, adhesion, and immune responses [17,18]. In pathological cells (e.g. cancer cells), certain saccharide receptors are overexpressed [19,20]. Semiconductor quantum dots have been used to visualize the distribution of these receptors [21–23]. However, these quantum dots are intrinsically toxic and much larger than the target molecules (hence potentially causing artificial clustering or altering trafficking dynamics of the targets). We thus envision that GQDs shall be better fluorescence reporters to reveal the distribution and dynamics of carbohydrate receptors.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can by synthesized using top-down exfoliation from larger carbon precursors (e.g., graphite powder [24], coal [25], carbon black [26], carbon fiber [27]) based on chemical, electrochemical, or physical methods. These top-down methods however produce GQDs with large heterogeneity. Alternatively, GQDs can be synthesized using “bottom-up” approaches based on fusion and carbonization of small organic molecules, which often can offer better production yield and homogeneity as compared to the top-down methods. Recently, Wang et al. demonstrated a bottom-up method using pyrene as the precursor to produce GQDs with good crystallinity and high production yield [28]. However, the obtained pyrene derived GQDs bear mainly –OH groups, which cannot be easily functionalized with biomolecules.

In this work (Scheme 1 ), we synthesize pyrene derived GQDs with –COOH groups which exhibit excitation independent green fluorescence, small and uniform size, higher photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) and ease to be functionalized as compared with pyrene derived OH-GQDs. And for the first time, GQDs are conjugated with monosaccharides (galactose and mannose) for specific imaging of carbohydrate receptors on cell membrane. Overexpression of mannose receptor (c-type lectin) in human breast cancer cells, overexpression of galactose receptor (asialoglycoprotein receptor, ASGPR) in human liver cancer cells as well as the endocytosis and exocytosis of activated ASGPR complexes are revealed. This study suggests the unique potential of GQDs in research of glycobiology.

The precursor, pyrene (0.5g, Sigma-Aldrich) was refluxed with hot nitric acid (40mL, 65%, Merck), and vigorously stirred for 12h at 80°C. After cooling to room temperature, the yellowish cloudy mixture was diluted with deionized water (1L) and filtered through a membrane filter (0.22µm, Millipore) to remove the acid, followed by drying in a vacuum chamber to obtain 1,3,6-trinitropyrene powder. The yield of trinitropyrene was calculated as ca. 54%. Subsequently, 80mg of the above product was dispersed in a NaHCO3 solution (0.08M, 15mL) and the dispersion was ultrasonicated for 1h. The resultant yellow suspension was then transferred into a 20mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 10h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained dark brown solution was filtered with a syringe filter (0.22µm, Millipore) to remove large particles, and further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (with cut-off molecular weight of 500Da, Spectrum) for 2days to remove ions and small molecules. Then the obtained solution was further ultrafiltered with a centrifuge filter (10kDa cut-off, Amicon-Ultra, Millipore) to remove large-sized pieces or aggregations. The remaining GQDs were freeze-dried into powders.

To prepare galactose-functionalized GQDs (Gal-GQDs), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 15mM, Sigma-Aldrich) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 22.5mM) were successively added to GQD solution (5mg/mL, diluted with phosphate buffer saline, pH 7). After 20min, the above solution was mixed with galactosamine (25mM, Sigma-Aldrich) under mild shaking for 4h. Subsequently, the mixture was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight of 500Da, Spectrum) for 48h to remove the unreacted reactants and ions. The same protocol was applied to conjugate mannosamine (25mM, Sigma-Aldrich) with GQDs (Man-GQDs).

The structure of GQDs were analyzed with atomic force microscopy (MFP-3D AFM microscope, Asylum research) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL 2100). Their chemical compositions were revealed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VG ESCALAB 220I-XL) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer FTIR Spectrum GX 69233). The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence properties of GQDs were characterized by a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) and a Fluorolog® PL spectrometer (Horiba), respectively.

Human breast cancer cells (MCF-7, ATCC), human liver cancer cells (HepG2, ATCC) and human cervical cancer cells (heLa, ATCC) were cultured with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Lonza) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco) and 1% penicillin streptomycin (PS). Human breast epithelial cells (MCF-10A, ATCC) were cultured with DMEM/F12 supplemented with 20% FBS and 1% PS. All the cells were incubated in an incubator at 37°C in an air atmosphere containing 5% CO2.

To conduct the imaging experiments, cells were seeded onto a Lab-Tek II chambered coverglass (Nunc) for overnight growth. Then Gal- /Man-GQDs (dispersed in DMEM, 120µg/mL) were applied to the cells 1h before imaging. Then the cells were rinsed and incubated with FluoroBrite DMEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Confocal images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Zeiss LSM 710) with a 63× oil objective lens. The time-lapse images (80 frames in 2min) were also recorded using an inverted total internal reflection fluorescence microscope (TIRFM) system (Zeiss) with a 100×/1.45 oil objective lens, after the cells were incubated with Gal-GQDs for 20min. All the images were processed with ImageJ.

The MTT (3-[4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was applied to justify the cytotoxicity of prepared GQDs. Three different cell lines were transferred into a 96-well plate at a cell density of around 5×103 cells/well. After 24h’ incubation in DMEM, cells were introduced with various concentrations of GQDs (100, 200, 300, and 400µg/mL) for 24h. Subsequently, after removing old media, the MTT solution (0.5mg/mL) was added to the wells for another 4h’ incubation. Then the solution was discarded and 100µL of isopropanol was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. After 20min shaking in darkness, the optical density (OD) of each well was recorded by a microplate reader at 570nm. The cell viability was calculated by following the equation:
(1)
Cell
viability
( % ) =
OD
of
the
cells
treated
with
GQDs
OD
of
controls
× 100
Where controls are the cells with no GQDs added and the cell viability of controls is recorded as 100%.

Wang et al. has demonstrated a bottom-up synthesis of GQDs in NaOH (0.2M) using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as the precursor, which fuses into single-layered graphene crystalline structure under hydrothermal condition. But such GQDs enriched with –OH groups cannot be readily functionalized. Here, we replaced NaOH with NaHCO3 (0.08M) to obtain GQDs with active –COOH groups. In the nucleophilic substitution reaction under hydrothermal condition, the positively charged –NO2 groups from trinitropyrene are substituted by alkaline groups (e.g., HCO3 −, CO3 2−, OH−), thus leading to grafting of COOH groups onto the GQD. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) reveals that the average diameter of the obtained COOH-GQDs is ∼5.4nm (±0.91, n=103) which is larger than the originally reported OH-GQDs (3.5±0.6nm). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) resolves the lattice spacing of ∼0.21nm corresponding to graphene (100) planes. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) shows that the average GQD thickness is ∼1.1nm (±0.46, n=157), indicating that most of them are single- or double-layered (Fig. 1c and d).

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of COOH-GQDs (Fig. 2 a) demonstrates the characteristic peaks originated from the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H bonds (∼3500cm−1), CO/CC bonds (∼1631cm−1), C–H/N–O bonds (∼1364cm−1), and C–O bonds (1092cm−1). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis indicates the abundant existence of oxygenated groups (Fig. 2b). COOH-GQDs are highly stable in aqueous solutions (no obvious aggregation is observed even after 6months). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be well-fitted with the characteristic peaks from CC bonds (284.5eV), C–O/C–N (285.6eV) and O–CO (288.7eV), while the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be de-convoluted into CO (532.6eV) and C–O (531.6eV) peaks. A small fraction (∼1.55%) of nitrogen is originated from C-NH2, C-NH-C and C-O bonds at 399.6eV, 400.2eV and 403.1eV respectively (Fig. 2e) [29,30]. Taken together, we can conclude that the obtained GQDs bear abundant –OH groups, some –COOH groups and few –NH2/−NO2 groups at the edges.

The aqueous dispersion of as-prepared GQDs appears brownish yellow under daylight (Fig. 3 a, inset) and shows bright green fluorescence under 365nm UV illumination (Fig. 3b, inset). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 3a) exhibits three shoulder peaks at ∼240nm (5.2eV), ∼380nm (3.3eV) and ∼450nm (2.8eV), which are produced by π–π∗ transition of aromatic CC bonds in the GQD basal plane, n1–σ1 ∗ transition of CO bonds at the edges, and n2-σ2 ∗ transition of C-O bonds at the edges, respectively (Fig. 3c) [31]. The maximum emission of COOH-GQD is attained at ∼535nm while being excited at ∼488nm (Fig. 3b). The emission at 535nm is originated from radiative relaxation of excited electron from π∗ to n1 orbital (Fig. 3c). In the other words, the emission property of COOH-GQD is dictated by the bandgap between π∗ and n1 orbitals. In comparison, OH-GQDs have a maximum emission peak at 520nm with 485nm excitation, corresponding to the bandgap between π∗ and n2 orbitals [28]. Our observation is consistent with the previous theoretical calculation which suggests that COOH group causes slightly more red-shifts than OH group at edges [32]. Notably, COOH-GQD not only can be easily conjugated with biomolecules but also exhibits significantly higher absolute quantum yield than that of OH-GQDs (31% vs. 23%). It is also noteworthy that the emission peak is essentially excitation independent (Fig. 3d), indicating the high homogeneity of pyrene-derived COOH-GQDs. The PL decay lifetime of COOH-GQDs is determined to be 3.7ns (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information). Furthermore, the as-prepared GQDs show low cytotoxicity to various cell types (MCF-7, HepG2 and HeLa), even at a high concentration of 0.4mg/mL which is much higher than the needed for bioimaging (Fig. 4 ).

Taken-together, the pyrene-derived COOH-GQDs are highly desirable for bioimaging because of high brightness, small size and molecular weight, excellent biocompatibility, and good photo-stability. A number of efforts have been made to functionalize GQDs with proteins in order to fluorescently enlighten the protein binding targets (e.g., protein receptors on cell membrane). We for the first time functionalize GQDs with monosaccharides aiming to specifically label carbohydrate receptors on cell membrane which play important physiological functions. Specifically, galactosamine or mannosamine is covalently conjugated to the COOH-GQDs via amide bonding. Subsequently, FTIR and UV–Vis absorption were conducted to verify the successful conjugation. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 5 a), two peaks at 1568 and 1250cm-1 appear after conjugation, indicating the presence of CO-NH and C-O-C bonding. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5b), a distinguished peak at around 260nm also appeared, which may originate from the n-σ∗ transition of N–CO bonds, testifying the successful conjugation of monosaccharide moieties. Notably, both the photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs don’t significantly alter after conjugation except slight redshift in emission (∼8nm) (Fig. S2 in SI).

Cancer cells often overexpress certain types of carbohydrate receptors (lectins). It is known that galactose receptors in hepatocytes involve in binding, internalization, and subsequent clearance of asialoglycoproteins (bearing galactose terminal) from the circulation. But their functions are still not completely understood. These receptors are more abundantly expressed in hepatoma cells (cancer liver cells) [19]. Galactose tagged GQDs (Gal-GQDs) can specifically interact and fluorescently lighten up the galactose receptors. Confocal fluorescence imaging reveals that indeed galactose receptors populate the membrane of HepG2 cells (human liver cancer cell line) whereas they are much less present in MCF-7 cells (human breast cancer cell line) and rarely found in Hela cells (human cell line derived from cervical cancer) and MCF-10A cells (non-tumor human breast epithelial cell line) (Fig. 6 ). Mannose tagged GQDs (Man-GQDs) can selectively label mannose receptors which are found in several cell types (e.g., macrophages) and play a role in both the innate and adaptive immune systems. It has been found that mannose receptors are overexpressed in human breast cancer cells [33,34]. Consistently, confocal imaging confirmed that mannose receptors are much expressed on plasma membrane of MCF-7 cells but not HepG2, Hela and MCF-10A cells. These experiments testify that sugar conjugated GQDs can be used to uncover the expression and distribution of carbohydrate receptors as well as to identify certain types of cancer cells (Fig. 6).

Just like protein receptors, binding with the corresponding ligand (here, or sugar residue) will trigger internalization of carbohydrate receptors through endocytosis. And after undertaking some trafficking steps, endocytosed receptors, if not being degraded, will eventually be recycled back to the cell membrane be ready for another round of stimulation. Although the trafficking (recycling) dynamics of many protein receptors has been extensively investigated, the dynamics of carbohydrate receptors is barely studied. Herein, we employed total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy (TIRFM) to reveal the dynamics of galactose receptors. With TIRFM, only the thin section (<200nm thick) including the cell membrane or the immediately adjacent subplasmalemmal region is illuminated and imaged. Therefore, it is a powerful tool to investigate membrane receptors and their turnover dynamics. The TIRFM images in Fig.7 a and b show the galactose receptor clusters labeled by Gal-GQDs on the plasma membrane of a HepG2 cell. As shown by the time-lapse imaging, the receptor cluster indicated by the arrow in Fig.7a is endocytosed (vesicular retrieval into the cell and hence moving away from the thin illumination zone, Fig.7e) as evidenced by fluorescence dimming and disappearance over time (4s or so). Time-lapse imaging in Fig.7e shows that the cluster indicated by the arrow in Fig.7b is exocytosed from a previously internalized receptor-containing vesicle as evidenced by fluorescence increase over time as the vesicle approaches the thin illumined subplasmalemmal region and eventually fuses with the plasma membrane.

In summary, we synthesized a new GQD via a modified hydrothermal route using pyrene as the precursor. The obtained GQDs are bright (QY ∼31%), mall size (∼5.4nm), easy to be covalently conjugated with biomolecules via its COOH groups, biocompatible, and highly soluble. For the first time, we functionalize GQDs with sugar moieties (galactose and mannose). Such sweet GQDs are instrumental to specifically label carbohydrate receptors and track their dynamics in real-time. This study opens a new perspective in the application of GQDs for bioimaging. While inheriting some intrinsic features from its 2D counterpart (graphene), GQDs with a number of new properties add a new dimension for graphene applications. Many 2D materials other than graphene are emerging [35–37]. Their 0D forms similarly shall provide vast new opportunities in electronics, energy, catalysis, imaging and sensing [38].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by an AcRF tier 2 grant (MOE2014-T2-1-003) from Ministry of Education (Singapore).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2017.08.006.
